title: That Weirdo Isn't Satoshi
tagline: Everyone thinks they know who he is. He really hasn't got a clue.
logline: A wry British comedy about Adam Back — a retired Hampstead librarian who has spent a decade being mistaken for Satoshi Nakamoto. He has never owned a computer. He thinks Bitcoin is the name of a racehorse. Directed in the Chris Morris / Armando Iannucci / Charlie Kaufman tradition of absurdist British satire.
synopsis: Adam Back — balding, spectacularly messy-haired, glasses permanently sliding off his nose — is a retired Hampstead librarian who has spent a decade being mistaken for Satoshi Nakamoto. He has never owned a computer. He thinks Bitcoin is the name of a racehorse. He charges the journalists camped outside his door two pounds a cup for tea. When a Lamborghini-driving American whale, a Russian oligarch's daughter by helicopter, two incompetent MI6 agents, and a chain-smoking French journalist all descend on NW3 at once, Adam is finally going to find out what on earth Bitcoin actually is. The big twist, in a rainy Hampstead garden at midnight: the real Satoshi is a painfully shy retired IT consultant from Croydon who just wanted to read in peace.
posterPrompt: Cinematic British comedy movie poster, portrait orientation. A balding middle-aged British man with wild uncombed black hair spraying sideways from the sides of his head, round wire-rimmed glasses slipping halfway down his nose, hollow cheeks, one eye wide and one squinting in bemused paranoia, rumpled greenish-grey collared shirt. Cream-coloured backdrop faintly overlaid with binary code and hexadecimal digits. Studio-lit, high-contrast, intelligent absurdist satire aesthetic. Title text space reserved at top. Tone: Chris Morris directs Charlie Kaufman, produced by Armando Iannucci. Deadpan, awkward, brilliant. No other people in frame.
clipPrompts: Cinematic 8-second clip in the style of Armando Iannucci's The Death of Stalin. Morning light on a Hampstead terraced street in NW3 London. Handheld tracking shot along a crowd of two dozen journalists camped on the pavement with microphones and cameras, British drizzle in the air. The red front door of number 42 opens a crack. A balding middle-aged British man — wild uncombed black hair spraying sideways from the sides of his otherwise bald head, round wire-rimmed glasses slipping halfway down his nose, rumpled greenish-grey collared shirt — peers out like a mole emerging into daylight, one eye wide, one squinting in bemused paranoia. Fast whip-zoom onto his face. Natural overcast light, realistic skin texture, muted British palette of slate grey and wet brick red. Ambient sound of drizzle and clicking shutters.

Cinematic 8-second clip in the Chris Morris / Brass Eye tradition. Cramped cluttered Hampstead kitchen, afternoon light through a net-curtained window. The same balding man — wild black hair sideways, glasses sliding down his nose, rumpled shirt — pours tea from a dented copper kettle into seven mismatched chipped mugs arranged on a wooden tray. Through the window, a phalanx of journalists waits. Close-up insert on his shaky hand scribbling 'TEA £2 A CUP' on a piece of cardboard with a biro. Cut wide as he nudges the back door open with his hip, tray balanced, cardigan pocket bulging with loose change, deadpan resigned expression. Warm kitchen light, natural hand-held camera, muted British suburban palette, dry comedy timing.

Cinematic 8-second clip, rapid-cut chaos montage in the Armando Iannucci / Veep style. Narrow Hampstead residential street outside the same red door. A bright yellow Lamborghini Urus screeches to a halt, blocking traffic. In the distance over the rooftops a black helicopter descends onto Hampstead Heath. Two British MI6 agents in badly-fitting charcoal suits clamber out of a Vauxhall Astra. A French journalist with a Gitane hanging from her lip sprints past with a boom mic. Middle of the frame — the balding librarian, wild black hair, glasses halfway down his nose, greenish-grey shirt, rumpled cardigan, frozen with a tray of teacups, mouth slightly open, eyes enormous behind the glasses, absolutely bewildered. Quick zoom-in on his face. Overcast grey British sky, slate and wet-brick palette, handheld camera, kinetic energy.

Cinematic 8-second clip, rainy midnight, Charlie Kaufman existential-twist tone. Interior of a cluttered Victorian Hampstead library study — floor-to-ceiling dark wood bookshelves, rain lashing a tall sash window, a single green banker's lamp lighting a cluttered desk piled with horse-racing newspapers. The balding librarian — wild black hair sprayed sideways, glasses slipping down his nose, rumpled cardigan — sits at the desk squinting at a copy of the Racing Post, a chipped mug of tea at his elbow. Standing awkwardly in the doorway behind him: a painfully shy, damp, muddy retired IT consultant from Croydon, late 50s, grey anorak, sensible trainers, holding a worn laptop bag — the real Satoshi. Long beat of silence. The librarian slowly turns, glasses sliding further down his nose. Slow dolly-in on both faces. Deep amber lamp glow, navy shadows, the sound of rain on glass.

cards.text: Everyone thinks they know who he is.
He really hasn't got a clue.
Dir. Chris Morris · Wr. Charlie Kaufman · Prod. Armando Iannucci

voScript: In Hampstead... one man... is about to find out what on earth Bitcoin actually is. He has never owned a computer. He thinks it is a racehorse. And the world... has come knocking. He is not the Bitcoin messiah. He is a very naughty librarian.
musicPrompt: Retro orchestral British comedy score, pizzicato strings, manic brass stabs, slightly ridiculous 1960s spy-caper feel mixed with Armando Iannucci's The Death of Stalin — plucked violins building tension, a walking bass, comedic french horn flourishes, dry and tight, thirty seconds, no vocals, upbeat paranoid energy.
